label: Product name
placeholder: Example: AeroPress Go Travel Coffee Maker
helper: Enter the exact product name or working name that should appear in the copy.
why: The product cannot be identified or titled accurately without its name.

label: Product format
placeholder: Select the product format
helper: This controls which product-detail fields and purchase information are relevant.
why: Physical, digital, subscription, and bundled products require different purchase information.

label: Product category
placeholder: Example: Portable coffee maker, skincare serum, online course
helper: Describe the product category clearly enough to establish context and relevant claim rules.
why: The category determines terminology, customer expectations, structure, and potential regulated-claim concerns.

label: Target customer
placeholder: Who is most likely to buy this product, and what matters to them?
helper: Describe the intended customer, situation, experience level, or buying priorities.
why: Product value must be translated into benefits that matter to a specific customer.

label: Primary goal
placeholder: Select the main goal
helper: Choose the main job the description should perform.
why: The goal affects emphasis, structure, detail level, CTA strength, and SEO treatment.

label: Essential product facts
placeholder: Explain what it is, how it works, its main components, and verified capabilities.
helper: Include only facts that may be stated publicly and accurately.
why: The description requires a reliable factual foundation and must not infer unsupported capabilities.

label: Main customer benefits
placeholder: What useful outcomes or improvements does the product provide?
helper: Focus on supported customer value rather than exaggerated promises.
why: Benefits explain why the supplied facts and features matter to the buyer.

label: Brand name
placeholder: Example: Northfield Supply
helper: Leave blank when the brand should not be mentioned.
why: The brand may need to appear in titles, descriptions, or CTA wording.

label: Store or destination
placeholder: Example: Shopify store, WooCommerce page, retail catalogue
helper: Name the intended store, page type, or sales environment when known.
why: The destination affects formatting, length, title style, SEO elements, and purchase-detail placement.

label: Tone
placeholder: Select a tone
helper: Choose the overall writing style without changing the underlying facts.
why: Tone materially changes phrasing and positioning while a safe default reduces user effort.

label: Output language
placeholder: Select the output language
helper: The complete generated package will use this language.
why: Language cannot always be inferred reliably from product data or the publishing destination.

label: Description length
placeholder: Select a preferred length
helper: Auto chooses an appropriate depth for the product, destination, and sales goal.
why: Different stores and purchase decisions require materially different description depths.

label: Key features
placeholder: List the most important functions, components, or design features.
helper: Include features that should appear separately from the main narrative.
why: A feature list supports scanning and prevents important capabilities from being buried.

label: Technical specifications
placeholder: Dimensions, weight, capacity, compatibility, power, model, or other specifications.
helper: Enter exact specifications only; unsupported values will not be estimated.
why: Physical products often require precise specifications before a customer can purchase confidently.
display: Show when productFormat is Physical product or Bundle.

label: Materials or ingredients
placeholder: List relevant materials, ingredients, composition, sourcing, or allergen details.
helper: Include exact terminology and any qualification that must remain attached to a claim.
why: Composition can affect product suitability, trust, safety, care, and purchase decisions.
display: Show when productFormat is Physical product or Bundle.

label: What is included
placeholder: List package contents, files, modules, accessories, licences, or bonuses.
helper: Clarify exactly what the customer receives and what is not included when important.
why: Clear package contents reduce misunderstanding and avoid implying that optional items are included.

label: Use, setup or care
placeholder: Explain setup, use, care, compatibility, prerequisites, or safe handling.
helper: Add only information that a buyer should know before or shortly after purchase.
why: Some products require setup, maintenance, compatibility, or safe-use context to be described accurately.

label: Approved proof and credentials
placeholder: Verified tests, certifications, awards, ratings, research, reviews, or measurable results.
helper: Include only approved evidence and enough context to state it accurately.
why: Evidence can strengthen credibility but must be supplied rather than invented.

label: Claims and restrictions
placeholder: Claims to avoid, required disclaimers, legal wording, limitations, or sensitive topics.
helper: Use this for regulated products, restricted claims, and important product limitations.
why: The product owner knows which statements are prohibited, qualified, jurisdiction-specific, or commercially sensitive.

label: Variants and options
placeholder: Sizes, colours, flavours, plans, editions, quantities, or customisation choices.
helper: Describe only variants that are currently available or intended for publication.
why: Variants may change purchase information, suitability wording, and product-title treatment.

label: Price or offer details
placeholder: Price, valid discount, bundle saving, tax note, or offer conditions.
helper: Leave blank to omit price and promotional claims.
why: Pricing and promotions must be supplied exactly and must never be fabricated.

label: Availability and delivery
placeholder: Stock status, processing time, delivery method, region, access timing, or fulfilment limits.
helper: Include only current or approved fulfilment information.
why: Delivery and availability can materially affect a buyer’s decision and must remain accurate.

label: Warranty and returns
placeholder: Warranty length, return period, exclusions, trial conditions, or support policy.
helper: State exact terms or leave blank; the model will not invent a policy.
why: Purchase protections and limitations should be described only from supplied policy details.

label: Digital access details
placeholder: File formats, download method, licence, device access, updates, or access duration.
helper: Explain how the customer receives and may use the digital product.
why: Digital products require access, format, licence, and compatibility information instead of physical shipping details.
display: Show when productFormat is Digital product or Bundle.

label: Subscription terms
placeholder: Billing frequency, renewal, cancellation, minimum term, trial, and included access.
helper: Include exact recurring-payment terms and avoid implied conditions.
why: Recurring products require transparent billing, renewal, cancellation, and access information.

label: Customer need or problem
placeholder: What situation, frustration, task, or need leads the customer to seek this product?
helper: Use a realistic customer need without exaggerating fear or discomfort.
why: The buying context helps connect the product to a relevant and credible customer need.

label: Key differentiators
placeholder: What makes this product meaningfully different from common alternatives?
helper: Use verifiable differences and avoid unsupported superiority claims.
why: Distinctive positioning cannot be inferred safely from generic category knowledge.

label: Use cases
placeholder: Describe realistic situations, environments, tasks, or customer scenarios.
helper: Include only uses the product is designed or approved for.
why: Concrete scenarios help customers assess fit without inventing unsupported applications.

label: Purchase concerns
placeholder: Common questions about fit, difficulty, price, compatibility, quality, or delivery.
helper: Add concerns the description should resolve without becoming defensive.
why: Known objections can be answered naturally using verified product information.

label: Search keywords
placeholder: Primary phrase and a few closely related terms, separated by commas.
helper: Leave blank for natural category language without deliberate keyword targeting.
why: User-supplied search terms help align the copy with an existing SEO strategy without keyword guessing.

label: Brand voice notes
placeholder: Describe preferred vocabulary, personality, formality, and phrases to use or avoid.
helper: Add guidance beyond the selected tone only when the brand has a distinct voice.
why: Specific brand conventions cannot be inferred from a general tone selection.

label: Additional requirements
placeholder: Add any important instruction not covered elsewhere.
helper: Use for essential context only; standard writing and proofreading rules are already applied.
why: One bounded catch-all field supports legitimate specialised requirements without creating several miscellaneous inputs.
